title: Victoria sandwich
subtitle: The classic British sponge with jam and cream
excerpt: A Victoria sandwich is two layers of equal-weight sponge, sandwiched with jam and whipped cream or buttercream. The recipe is the foundational British cake: equal weights of butter, sugar, eggs, and flour. The result should be level, springy, and light, with a close, even crumb and no sunken centre.
sourceNotes: The Victoria sandwich is named for Queen Victoria and appears in Victorian domestic cookery manuals. Isabella Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg, gives the standard formula: equal weights of butter, sugar, and flour, with eggs for structure. The classic British bake has remained unchanged since the mid-nineteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled, cooled sponges wrapped individually. Fill after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the sponges up to a day ahead; fill just before serving.

content.text: A Victoria sandwich is the British standard sponge: equal weights of butter, sugar, flour, and eggs, beaten together and baked in two tins. The two sponges are sandwiched with jam and either whipped cream or buttercream, then dusted with icing sugar. The structure of the cake is the air beaten into the butter; the baking powder lifts it and levels the top.
Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases with baking paper.

Beat the softened butter and caster sugar together for 4 to 5 minutes until pale, very fluffy, and almost white. This is where the air goes in; underbeating produces a dense sponge. Add the eggs one at a time, beating well after each addition. If the mixture starts to curdle, add a tablespoon of the flour. Add the vanilla. Sift in the flour and baking powder, then fold in with a large metal spoon until just combined. Add the milk and fold again; the batter should drop slowly from the spoon.

Divide the batter equally between the two tins and level the tops. Bake for 22 to 25 minutes. The sponges are done when they are evenly golden on top, spring back when pressed lightly in the centre, and have pulled away slightly from the sides of the tin. Cool in the tins for 5 minutes, then turn out onto a wire rack and leave to cool completely before filling.

Place one sponge flat-side up on a serving plate. Spread the jam to the edges, then spoon on the whipped cream and smooth it gently. Place the second sponge on top, flat-side down. Dust with icing sugar just before serving.
